title: Robot Control Modes: Open-Loop · Closed-Loop · Teleoperated

zones.label: Open-Loop Control
zones.summary: Pre-programmed timed sequences — no sensor feedback
facts: Definition: the robot executes a fixed sequence of motor commands with no awareness of the result
Sensor needed: none — the program controls only timing
MicroPython pattern: motor_on(); time.sleep(1); motor_off()
Example: drive forward 2 s, turn left 0.5 s, repeat — robot dance routine
Reliability: errors accumulate over time — wheel slip, battery drop, floor friction cause drift
The robot cannot detect or correct for obstacles, wheel slip, or surface changes
Advantage: simplest code; no sensor wiring required; great for scripted demonstrations
Disadvantage: drifts off course over time; no ability to react to the environment
Used in Chapter 10: timed motor patterns, robot dance sequences
Real-world analogy: a music box — plays the same sequence every time regardless of the audience
Best for: scripted demonstrations, dance routines, simple intro labs

zones.label: Closed-Loop Control
zones.summary: Sensor feedback drives decisions — the robot adapts to its environment
facts: Definition: the robot reads sensors, compares the reading to a goal, and adjusts motors to reduce error
Feedback cycle: Sense → Compare → Act → Repeat (runs continuously)
Sensor needed: distance sensor (ToF, ultrasonic) or line-following IR pair
MicroPython pattern: while True: d = tof.read(); if d < 20: turn(); else: forward()
Example: collision avoidance — stop and turn if ToF reads < 20 cm
Example: line following — if left IR detects line, turn left; if right IR detects, turn right
Reliability: self-correcting; adapts to obstacles, surface changes, and battery drop
Advantage: autonomous — robot responds to real-world conditions without human input
Disadvantage: more complex code; sensor noise can cause erratic behavior without filtering
Used in Chapter 10: collision avoidance algorithm, dual-sensor line follower
Best for: autonomous navigation, obstacle avoidance, any behavior that must adapt to the world

zones.label: Teleoperated Control
zones.summary: A human operator sends real-time commands via WiFi or BLE
facts: Definition: a human pilot sends movement commands in real time over a wireless link
Sensor needed: the wireless radio (WiFi or BLE); distance sensors optional for safety
WiFi method: robot runs an HTTP web server; browser sends GET/POST requests to control motors
WiFi round-trip latency: ~7–30 ms on a local LAN (Pico W measured)
BLE method: phone or another Pico W sends BLE GATT characteristic writes to drive motors
BLE round-trip latency: ~1–10 ms (faster than WiFi for direct peer-to-peer commands)
MicroPython: parse incoming HTTP request or BLE packet; map command to motor action
WiFi requires: both robot and browser on the same network via an access point (router)
BLE requires: no router — device connects directly to robot (peer-to-peer)
Advantage: human judgment guides the robot; excellent for exploration or competition
Disadvantage: requires wireless signal; limited by operator reaction time and range
Used in Chapter 11 (WiFi web server) and Chapter 12 (BLE central/peripheral)
Best for: robot competitions, remote inspection, demos where a human steers the action

quiz.question: Which control mode uses no sensors and relies entirely on pre-timed motor commands?
quiz.explanation: Open-loop control executes a fixed sequence of timed commands with no feedback from the environment. The robot dance routine is a classic open-loop program — it runs the same way every time, regardless of what obstacles or conditions it encounters.

quiz.question: Which control mode reads sensor data and changes motor behavior based on what the sensor detects?
quiz.explanation: Closed-loop control creates a feedback cycle: Sense → Compare → Act → Repeat. The robot measures distance (or line position), compares it to a threshold, and adjusts motors accordingly. Collision avoidance and line following are both closed-loop behaviors.

quiz.question: Which control mode requires a human pilot sending real-time commands over WiFi or Bluetooth?
quiz.explanation: Teleoperated control puts a human in the loop: the operator sends forward/turn/stop commands via a web browser (WiFi) or a BLE connection. The robot executes those commands in real time with latency of 7–30 ms (WiFi) or 1–10 ms (BLE).

quiz.question: Which control mode causes the robot to drift off course over time because it cannot detect wheel slip or battery drop?
quiz.explanation: Open-loop robots run fixed timed sequences. When one wheel is slightly faster, the battery drops, or the floor surface changes, there is no correction mechanism — errors accumulate and the robot drifts from its intended path.

quiz.question: In Chapter 10, the collision avoidance robot stops and turns when the ToF sensor reads less than 20 cm. What control mode is this?
quiz.explanation: The collision avoidance algorithm reads a real sensor (ToF distance), compares it to a threshold (20 cm), and changes motor behavior (stop, reverse, turn). That Sense → Compare → Act → Repeat cycle is the definition of closed-loop control.
